evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations in FASTKD5 are associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency and early- to late-onset Leigh syndrome.
evidence.snippet: a progressive neurodegenerative disease characterized by lesions in the brainstem and basal ganglia

nodes.description: Biallelic FASTKD5 variants reduce the steady-state level of FASTKD5, a mitochondrial protein essential for processing mRNAs at non-canonical cleavage sites in the polycistronic primary mitochondrial transcript. Defective processing impairs maturation of the mtDNA-encoded COX1 mRNA, so translation of the core catalytic subunit COX1 falls and assembly of Complex IV is impaired, producing an isolated Complex IV biogenesis failure. This situates the lesion at the mitochondrial mRNA-processing step, upstream of COX1 translation and holoenzyme assembly — distinct from the assembly-factor, metallochaperone, and structural-subunit defects that underlie most other nuclear-type COX deficiencies.
nodes.id: FASTKD5 Loss and Defective Mitochondrial mRNA Processing

biological_processes: mitochondrial respiratory chain complex IV assembly

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations in FASTKD5 are associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency and early- to late-onset Leigh syndrome.
evidence.snippet: compound heterozygous variants of unknown significance in FASTKD5, a gene that codes for a mitochondrial protein essential for processing mRNAs at non-canonical cleavage sites in the primary mitochondrial transcript, in three subjects with Leigh syndrome

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations in FASTKD5 are associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency and early- to late-onset Leigh syndrome.
evidence.snippet: reduced steady-state levels of FASTKD5 protein by immunoblot, reduced translation of the cytochrome c oxidase subunit 1, impaired assembly of complex IV, and a consequent decrease in cytochrome c oxidase enzymatic activity

nodes.description: The bioenergetic deficit manifests in high-demand tissue. In FASTKD5-related disease the dominant phenotype is Leigh syndrome — a progressive neurodegenerative disease characterized by lesions in the brainstem and basal ganglia — with an onset that ranges from early to late. The extent of the molecular Complex IV deficiency correlates with the severity of the clinical phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations in FASTKD5 are associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency and early- to late-onset Leigh syndrome.
evidence.snippet: three subjects with Leigh syndrome, a progressive neurodegenerative disease characterized by lesions in the brainstem and basal ganglia

evidence.reference_title: Bi-allelic mutations in FASTKD5 are associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency and early- to late-onset Leigh syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The extent of these deficiencies appeared to correlate with the severity of the clinical phenotype

nodes.description: The reduced amount of assembled Complex IV blocks electron transfer from cytochrome c to molecular oxygen and the coupled proton pumping, lowering oxidative ATP synthesis. In FASTKD5-deficient patient fibroblasts the cytochrome c oxidase enzymatic activity is decreased, consistent with the upstream assembly defect.
nodes.id: Impaired Terminal Electron Transfer and ATP Synthesis

biological_processes: mitochondrial electron transport, cytochrome c to oxygen
